ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Dal Khichdi; A0CKM (rice-based mixed dish with legumes) used as closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Dal Khichdi as of 2026.

main_ingredients: yellow lentils (moong dal or toor dal)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Dal Khichdi is approximately 250 g (one medium bowl), as commonly served in Indian restaurants and hawker stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Dal Khichdi — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Dal Khichadi (G00011044), confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from ICMR-NIN reference for comparable dal khichdi preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice-lentil mixed dish (khichdi analogue)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No exact GI entry for Dal Khichdi in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 51 estimated from the closest approved analogous entry: rice-lentil mixed dish (khichdi-type), which yields GI ~50–54 in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2021; Foster-Powell et al. 2002). GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (51 × 30.8) / 100 ≈ 15.7, rounded to 15.8. Lentils lower the overall GI relative to plain rice (GI ~72) due to high fibre and protein content.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 383 mg/100 g is borderline high; partially offset by potassium at 292 mg/100 g (Na:K ratio ~1.3:1)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced salt during preparation to bring sodium below 300 mg/100 g, and limit portion size to 200 g to keep per-meal sodium under 770 mg; the potassium content (292 mg/100 g) provides partial cardiovascular offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~51), high dietary fibre (3.4 g/100 g), and moderate protein (3.68 g/100 g) slow postprandial glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dal Khichdi is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic index (~51) and high fibre content; a standard 250 g serving yields a glycemic load of approximately 15–16, which is within the moderate range — portion control and increasing the lentil-to-rice ratio can further reduce glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.8 g/100 g), zero trans fat, high dietary fibre (3.4 g/100 g), and cholesterol at 0 mg/100 g support a heart-healthy profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dal Khichdi is broadly suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease given its negligible cholesterol, low saturated fat, and high soluble fibre from lentils which supports LDL reduction; sodium at 383 mg/100 g warrants portion moderation and a low-salt preparation request.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Turmeric (curcumin) provides anti-inflammatory benefit; lentils supply fibre >3 g/100 g supporting gut microbiome immune regulation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dal Khichdi is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory properties of turmeric (curcumin) and high dietary fibre; individuals with nightshade sensitivity should request omission of green chillies, and ghee should be replaced with plant oil to minimise saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Khichdi (also spelled Dal Khichadi or Dhal Khichdi) is a traditional Indian one-pot comfort dish made by slow-cooking rice and lentils (typically yellow moong dal or toor dal) together until they form a soft, porridge-like consistency. The dish is finished with a tadka — a tempering of ghee or oil with cumin seeds, turmeric, green chillies, onion, and tomato — which infuses the dish with aromatic depth. The texture is thick and creamy, the flavour is mildly spiced and earthy, and the colour is golden-yellow from turmeric. Dal Khichdi is widely consumed across India as a wholesome everyday meal and is considered one of the most digestible and nourishing dishes in Indian cuisine. In Singapore, it is commonly served in Indian restaurants and hawker stalls as a lunch or dinner option. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 111 kcal, 3.68 g protein, 5.65 g fat, 12.33 g carbohydrate, 3.4 g dietary fibre, and 383 mg sodium. The high fibre and protein content from lentils gives it a low glycemic index (~51), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-compatible. Sodium is borderline elevated and patients with hypertension should request a reduced-salt preparation. Turmeric provides curcumin, a well-studied anti-inflammatory compound relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management.
common_user_queries: Is dal khichdi good for diabetes?
Is dal khichdi suitable for hypertension?
What is dal khichdi made of?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database (G00011044) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from ICMR-NIN reference for comparable dal khichdi preparation. GI estimated from closest approved analogous entry in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. All mandatory multilingual names present for Indian cuisine served in Singapore.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific dal khichdi preparation is not reported in the HPB Foodo database (G00011044) or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported by HPB for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry for this dish. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero — traditional preparation uses ghee or vegetable oil with no industrial trans fats). cholesterol_mg reported as 0.0 per HPB source (confirmed zero). saturated_fat_g of 1.8 g/100 g is an estimate based on ICMR-NIN data for comparable dal khichdi with ghee; not directly reported by HPB. GI of 51 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 for rice-lentil mixed dish; no exact entry for Dal Khichdi exists in the database. All core macros (protein_g 3.68, fat_g 5.65, carbohydrate_g 12.33) are non-zero and sourced from HPB.
